refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output to support evaluation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the capability to measure/report energy efficiency metrics, and (2) the achievement of a target value. The refined FR focuses on the functional requirement—providing visibility and data—which is solution-neutral and within system control. Achievement of the target is a performance constraint/acceptance criterion, not a functional requirement. This separation clarifies that the system must instrument and expose energy data; whether the target is met depends on design choices (DP/IM) and operational context.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Demonstrate that the system can continuously or periodically measure energy_used (kWh or equivalent) and work_output (tons moved or equivalent task units), compute energy_per_ton, and expose this metric via operator interface, logs, or telemetry. Verify accuracy of measurement against calibrated instruments. Confirm data is available for post-evaluation analysis over the agreed evaluation period.
sub_frs: Measure total energy consumed by the NGHE solution during operation.
Measure total work output (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, or task-specific units) during operation.
Calculate and store energy efficiency metric (energy per unit output) at defined intervals.
Provide access to energy efficiency data for analysis and reporting to stakeholders.

candidate_dps: DP1: Instrument work output (tonnage moved) and energy input (power consumed) at independent measurement points; aggregate and compute ratio (energy/ton) at a centralized analytics layer; compare against target threshold and generate compliance reports.

DP2: Deploy real-time energy metering on the prime mover (motor/engine) and integrate with a work-output sensor (load cell, flow meter, or displacement counter); stream both signals to a local controller that computes and logs energy_per_ton continuously; trigger alerts if threshold exceeded.

DP3: Establish energy consumption baseline via controlled test cycles under known load conditions; apply regression model to predict energy_per_ton from operational parameters (speed, load, ambient conditions); validate predictions against periodic spot measurements; report modeled efficiency against target.

DP4: Decompose energy measurement into sub-system contributions (e.g., prime mover, hydraulics, auxiliary systems) and work output into task-specific components (e.g., payload moved, distance traveled); compute efficiency ratio per subsystem-task pair; aggregate to system-level energy_per_ton and compare to target.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1: Instrument work output (tonnage moved) and energy input (power consumed) at independent measurement points; aggregate and compute ratio (energy/ton) at a centralized analytics layer; compare against target threshold and generate compliance reports.

potential_sources: Measurement independence: Energy and work-output sensors operate on separate physical/logical channels, reducing cross-talk and calibration coupling.
Computation decoupling: Ratio calculation and reporting are logically separated from measurement acquisition, allowing independent evolution of analytics logic.
Temporal decoupling: Centralized aggregation permits asynchronous collection and batch processing, reducing real-time synchronization constraints.
Minor risk: Sensor calibration drift and time-alignment of asynchronous measurements could introduce systematic bias if not managed; mitigated by periodic cross-validation.
coupling_check.notes: DP1 avoids tight coupling between measurement hardware and control logic (DP2 risk), and avoids model-dependent predictions (DP3 risk). DP4 introduces unnecessary subsystem decomposition complexity without improving independence. DP1 is preferred for clarity, modularity, and traceability.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Work output (tonnage moved) can be measured directly or inferred reliably from available operational signals (e.g., load cell, GPS displacement, material flow rate). Risk: If work output is ambiguous or multi-modal (e.g., partial loads, idle time), measurement error propagates into energy_per_ton ratio. Mitigation: Define work-output measurement protocol in operational procedures; validate against independent audit.

Assumption: Energy input is measurable at a single point (e.g., fuel consumption, electrical power draw). Risk: If energy is distributed across multiple subsystems (e.g., prime mover + auxiliary hydraulics), single-point measurement may miss parasitic losses. Mitigation: Conduct energy balance audit; if subsystem decomposition is required, escalate to DP4.

Assumption: Evaluation period is long enough to average transient effects and operational variability. Risk: Short evaluation windows or non-representative duty cycles may yield energy_per_ton that does not reflect typical performance. Mitigation: Agree on evaluation period and duty-cycle definition with customer; document in acceptance criteria.

Assumption: Centralized analytics layer has sufficient data integrity and audit trail to support compliance reporting. Risk: Data loss, tampering, or clock skew could invalidate reported energy_per_ton. Mitigation: Implement data logging with checksums and timestamping; establish data governance policy aligned with worksite safety and operational standards.

Assumption: Target threshold E_target is stable and well-defined over the evaluation period. Risk: If target is ambiguous or subject to change, compliance determination becomes contentious. Mitigation: Formalize E_target in contract; establish change-control process if target revision is needed.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): Enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output (energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved) to support evaluation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets, with measurement independence, data integrity, and audit traceability to satisfy worksite safety and operational compliance requirements. DP (refined): Instrument work output and energy input at independent measurement points; aggregate signals and compute energy_per_ton ratio at a centralized analytics layer with data logging and timestamping; compare against agreed target threshold E_target over the defined evaluation period; generate compliance reports with audit trail. IM (preliminary): Energy measurement module (sensor + signal conditioning), work-output measurement module (sensor + signal conditioning), centralized data aggregation and analytics module (controller or edge compute), reporting and audit-log module (database + reporting interface).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized analytics layer that aggregates telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, computes energy efficiency metrics (energy/ton), compares against compliance thresholds, and generates compliance reports.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Receives work output (tonnage moved) and energy input (power consumed) measurements from Vehicle and Teleoperation modules at independent measurement points.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Transmits compliance violation alerts and threshold breach notifications to Safety Services when energy efficiency falls below target.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Sends compliance reports and efficiency metrics to operational tasking systems for decision-making and resource allocation.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment. NGHE Solution is the natural home for centralized analytics, aggregation logic, threshold comparison, and report generation. Telemetry ingestion and compliance reporting are core to a solution-level analytics layer. Minimal diffusion: only telemetry input and alert/tasking outputs cross boundaries.

Poor containment. Vehicle is a physical asset responsible for work execution and local sensing. Placing centralized analytics, aggregation, and compliance logic here violates separation of concerns. Energy/ton computation and threshold comparison would diffuse across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. Vehicle should only provide raw telemetry, not analytics.

Poor containment. Teleoperation manages remote control and operator interface, not analytics infrastructure. Placing aggregation and compliance logic here couples operator control with backend analytics, creating tight coupling and diffusing responsibility. Threshold comparison and reporting belong at solution level, not in teleoperation subsystem.

Very poor containment. Safety Services is responsible for safety alerts and hazard mitigation, not energy efficiency analytics or compliance reporting. Placing measurement aggregation and energy/ton computation here conflates safety concerns with operational efficiency metrics. High diffusion: would require Safety Services to ingest telemetry, compute metrics, and generate non-safety reports.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The DP is best contained within NGHE Solution because: (1) centralized analytics and aggregation are solution-level concerns, not subsystem concerns; (2) energy efficiency metrics and compliance thresholds are operational KPIs managed at the solution layer; (3) telemetry ingestion from independent measurement points (Vehicle and Teleoperation) naturally flows to a central analytics hub; (4) compliance reporting is a solution-level output artifact; (5) coupling is minimal—only telemetry input and alert/tasking outputs cross module boundaries.
containment_risks: Risk: Telemetry schema evolution. If Vehicle or Teleoperation change their measurement formats or add new sensors, the analytics layer must adapt. Mitigation: define a stable telemetry contract/interface that decouples measurement format from analytics logic.
Risk: Threshold configuration drift. If compliance thresholds are hardcoded in NGHE Solution, changes require redeployment. Mitigation: externalize thresholds to a configuration service or database.

Risk: Latency in aggregation. If real-time compliance alerting is required, centralized aggregation may introduce unacceptable delay. Mitigation: implement edge analytics in Vehicle or Teleoperation for critical thresholds, with NGHE Solution performing post-hoc validation and reporting.

Risk: Measurement point independence. If Vehicle and Teleoperation measurements are not synchronized or calibrated, energy/ton ratio may be inaccurate. Mitigation: establish measurement synchronization protocol and calibration procedures at the telemetry interface.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP1 directly and completely addresses the FR. The DP specifies independent measurement of work output (tonnage) and energy input (power), centralized aggregation, ratio computation (energy/ton), threshold comparison, and compliance reporting. This logically satisfies the requirement to enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output against customer-defined targets. The DP is solution-agnostic and does not prematurely constrain implementation.

diffusion_risks: Telemetry schema evolution: Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems may change measurement formats or add sensors. Mitigation: establish a stable, versioned telemetry contract at the input interface to decouple measurement format from analytics logic.

Threshold configuration drift: Hardcoded compliance thresholds in NGHE Solution require redeployment for changes. Mitigation: externalize thresholds to a configuration service or database, enabling runtime updates without code changes.

Real-time compliance alerting latency: Centralized aggregation may introduce unacceptable delay if immediate threshold breach alerts are required. Mitigation: implement edge analytics in Vehicle or Teleoperation for critical thresholds, with NGHE Solution performing post-hoc validation and reporting.

Measurement point synchronization and calibration: If Vehicle and Teleoperation measurements are not synchronized or calibrated, energy/ton ratio accuracy is compromised. Mitigation: establish measurement synchronization protocol and calibration procedures at the telemetry interface contract.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution exhibits excellent containment (0.92 score) for this DP. Centralized analytics, aggregation, metric computation, threshold comparison, and compliance reporting are naturally solution-level concerns. The module boundary is clean: telemetry ingestion (in) and compliance alerts/tasking outputs (out) are the only cross-boundary flows. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services all show poor containment (0.35–0.25) for this DP, as placing analytics logic in these subsystems would violate separation of concerns, create tight coupling, and diffuse responsibility across multiple modules. The ownership allocation to NGHE Solution is architecturally sound.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target over evaluation period) is directly measurable and verifiable. NGHE Solution's role as the centralized analytics layer provides a single point of truth for metric computation and compliance determination. Telemetry inputs from Vehicle and Teleoperation are independent measurement points, reducing single-point-of-failure risk. Compliance reports generated by NGHE Solution serve as the primary verification artifact.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The FR is allocated to NGHE Solution with well-defined responsibilities: (1) ingest telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation; (2) compute energy/ton ratio; (3) compare against E_target threshold; (4) generate compliance reports. External interfaces are explicit: SafetyAlert (out) for threshold breaches, Tasking (out) for operational reporting, and Telemetry (in) for measurement data. Ownership and accountability are unambiguous.

recommended_revisions: Define a stable, versioned telemetry interface contract specifying measurement schema, units, precision, and synchronization requirements. This decouples Vehicle and Teleoperation measurement formats from NGHE Solution analytics logic.

Externalize compliance thresholds (E_target) to a configuration service or database rather than hardcoding in NGHE Solution. This enables runtime threshold updates without redeployment and supports multi-customer or multi-worksite scenarios.

Establish a measurement synchronization and calibration protocol at the telemetry interface. Document timestamp alignment, sensor calibration procedures, and validation rules to ensure energy/ton ratio accuracy.

Evaluate latency requirements for compliance alerting. If real-time alerts are critical, implement edge analytics in Vehicle or Teleoperation for threshold detection, with NGHE Solution performing post-hoc validation and historical reporting.

Document the evaluation period definition and aggregation window (e.g., per-shift, per-day, per-project) in the acceptance criteria. Clarify whether energy_per_ton is computed as a rolling average, cumulative total, or discrete period snapshot.

Specify the compliance report format, distribution channels, and stakeholder recipients (e.g., customer, safety team, operations). Ensure SafetyAlert and Tasking outputs are aligned with downstream consumer expectations.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The DP is logically sound, the IM allocation to NGHE Solution is architecturally clean with excellent containment (0.92), and verification is feasible. However, the high safety criticality of energy consumption compliance and the identified diffusion risks—particularly measurement synchronization, threshold configuration drift, and real-time alerting latency—warrant decomposition into sub-FRs to address these concerns at the next level. Decomposition will expose critical interface contracts, edge analytics requirements, and configuration management patterns that must be designed and verified before implementation. The recommended revisions are architectural in nature and best resolved through sub-FR derivation rather than revision of the current level.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1.1: Ingest and validate telemetry measurements (work output tonnage and energy input power) from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems with synchronized timestamps, calibrated units, and schema conformance to a stable interface contract.

FR1.2: Compute energy consumption per unit work output (energy/ton ratio) from validated telemetry using aggregation windows (e.g., per-shift, per-day, per-project) and handle missing or anomalous data points without compromising metric accuracy.

FR1.3: Compare computed energy/ton ratio against customer-defined compliance thresholds (E_target) and generate compliance determination (pass/fail/warning) with supporting evidence and evaluation period metadata.

FR1.4: Detect energy consumption threshold breaches in real-time or near-real-time and issue SafetyAlert notifications to Safety Services with sufficient latency to enable operational response, while maintaining post-hoc validation and historical audit trail.

FR1.5: Externalize and manage compliance threshold configuration (E_target values, evaluation periods, alert latency tolerances) via a runtime-updatable service or database, enabling multi-customer and multi-worksite scenarios without code redeployment.

FR1.6: Generate and distribute compliance reports to authorized stakeholders (customer, safety team, operations) in a standardized format with metric summaries, threshold comparisons, breach events, and corrective action recommendations.

revise.why: No revision of current-level FR, DP, or IM is required. All gates pass. The recommended revisions from the gate analysis are best addressed through sub-FR decomposition and detailed design of interfaces, configuration management, and edge analytics patterns.

assumptions: Telemetry from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems is available with sufficient frequency and precision to compute energy/ton ratio accurately.
Customer-defined compliance thresholds (E_target) are stable or change infrequently enough to support externalization to a configuration service.
Safety Services can consume SafetyAlert outputs asynchronously; real-time synchronous alerting is not a hard requirement (to be validated in FR1.4 decomposition).
NGHE Solution has sufficient computational capacity and network bandwidth to aggregate telemetry from multiple vehicles and worksites without unacceptable latency.
Measurement synchronization and calibration protocols can be established and enforced at the telemetry interface contract without requiring changes to Vehicle or Teleoperation subsystem internals.
open_risks: Telemetry schema evolution: Vehicle and Teleoperation may change measurement formats or add sensors, breaking NGHE Solution's ingestion logic. Mitigation: establish versioned telemetry contract with backward compatibility rules (FR1.1).

Measurement synchronization and calibration: Timestamp misalignment or sensor calibration drift between Vehicle and Teleoperation measurements will degrade energy/ton ratio accuracy. Mitigation: define synchronization protocol and calibration procedures at interface (FR1.1).

Real-time alerting latency: Centralized aggregation may introduce unacceptable delay for threshold breach detection. Mitigation: evaluate latency requirements and implement edge analytics in Vehicle or Teleoperation if needed (FR1.4).
Threshold configuration drift: Hardcoded thresholds require redeployment for changes. Mitigation: externalize to configuration service (FR1.5).
Data quality and anomaly handling: Missing, duplicated, or anomalous telemetry points may skew energy/ton ratio computation. Mitigation: define data validation and anomaly handling rules in FR1.2.

Compliance report stakeholder alignment: Report format and distribution channels may not match downstream consumer expectations. Mitigation: clarify stakeholder requirements and report schema in FR1.6.
evidence_needed: Telemetry interface contract specification: measurement schema, units, precision, timestamp synchronization requirements, and versioning strategy.
Measurement synchronization and calibration protocol: timestamp alignment procedures, sensor calibration baseline, drift detection rules, and validation acceptance criteria.
Latency requirements analysis: acceptable delay for threshold breach detection and SafetyAlert issuance; determination of whether edge analytics is required.
Configuration management design: externalized threshold storage (database, service, or configuration file), update mechanisms, multi-customer/multi-worksite isolation, and audit trail.
Data quality and anomaly handling rules: missing data imputation strategy, outlier detection thresholds, and impact on energy/ton ratio accuracy.
Aggregation window definition: per-shift, per-day, per-project, or rolling window; handling of partial periods and period boundaries.
Compliance report format and distribution specification: stakeholder recipients, report schema, breach event details, and corrective action recommendations.
Real-time vs. post-hoc alerting trade-off analysis: latency, accuracy, and operational response requirements.
